data.title: Trump pardoned him, and he's ready to go back to prison
data.description:
On the evening of Jan. 23, 2025, Will Goodman heard his name rise above the noise of the prison dayroom. Once. Then again. Then a third time, unmistakable.

“Goodman — get up here. The president is pardoning you.”

Goodman had been convicted in federal court for his role in a case that unfolded in Washington, D.C. Prosecutors described it as coordinated and dangerous. FBI agents had carried out pre-dawn raids across multiple states, weapons drawn. Goodman had been sentenced to 27 months in federal prison. Several of those charged alongside him received even longer terms.

Around him, inmates drifted toward the televisions. The executive order crawled across the screen. The pardon was real.

By 2 a.m., correctional officers were walking Goodman out of the unit. Men he had lived beside for months clapped and shouted as he passed. He stepped into the freezing Connecticut night clutching a prison ID, using a walker and wearing a neck brace from a serious fall earlier in his sentence.

But no, Goodman had not been accused of storming the U.S. Capitol. His crime involved blocking access to a Washington abortion clinic, to stop what he believes is a more elemental injustice — the deliberate taking of innocent human life through abortion.

The next morning, he found a nearby Catholic church. After 20 months of fluorescent lights and clanging steel, stepping into the church felt like crossing into another world — incense, stained glass, stillness. “It was like stepping into heaven,” he told me.

After mass, he was driven to a nearby abortion clinic. Goodman prayed outside for a few minutes, then went to breakfast — his first real meal in nearly two years.

A few days later, he was sitting at the National Prayer Breakfast in Washington, surrounded by members of Congress, freed Israeli hostages, and President Trump’s extended family. From the podium, President Trump acknowledged Goodman and his co-defendants. The room stood and applauded.

The pardon removed him from prison. It did not alter the course of his life. I’ve known Goodman for more than 25 years, long enough to recognize that what followed was simply a continuation.

Goodman did not grow up especially religious or political. As an undergraduate at the University of Illinois, he was an engineering major and an extreme skier — drawn to risk for its own sake. After an accident, he switched to philosophy and began attending Bible study at the campus Newman Center.

Two documentaries shifted his trajectory: “The Silent Scream” and “The Hard Truth” showed footage of abortions at various stages of pregnancy. I was in a state of shock,” Goodman told me. “The victim was invisible to me. I didn’t even think there was a victim with abortion.”

He joined a campus pro-life group. At World Youth Day in 1993, Pope John Paul II told the crowd, “Young people, defend life.” Goodman took it as a directive.

By 1996, he had committed himself to pro-life work full-time. For years, that meant prayer vigils, sidewalk counseling, and protests. Over time, he came to believe that detachment itself was a moral failure. He studied the civil rights movement — the sit-ins, the willingness to accept arrest as witness.

“When a life is in danger,” he said, “intervening should come naturally.”

He is adamant about nonviolence. “How can you use violence when you’re trying to say life is sacred?” A mentor once told him: We’re going to rescue, and we’re going to be willing to lay our lives down. “The cross,” Goodman said, “is a more powerful weapon than any bomb.”

In 2017, he helped launch Red Rose Rescue, a network meant to avoid the sweeping federal prosecutions that had crushed earlier rescue movements. Activists enter clinic waiting rooms, offer women roses and information about alternatives, and refuse to leave. The goal is presence rather than confrontation.

Even within the pro-life movement, the tactic is disputed. Goodman frames it less as a strategy than an obligation: if a child were drowning, would trespass be justified? If the law declared certain children unworthy of rescue, would obedience still be moral?

For Goodman, there is no other choice.

In October 2020, Goodman and a small group entered a Washington, D.C., abortion clinic known for performing late-term procedures. Some sat in the waiting room praying, blocking a door; others, including Goodman, remained in the hallway offering information. “For him to get past me,” Goodman said later of the abortionist, “he would have had to go around me. I wasn’t going to block him. I was going to kneel and pray.”

Police eventually arrived, followed by FBI agents who filmed the group and conducted interrogations. After several hours, everyone was released. No charges were filed at the time.

Eighteen months later, the response came. In May 2022, the FBI carried out coordinated pre-dawn raids across seven states, entering homes with weapons drawn. Some of those arrested had not even been present at the original protest. “They raided grandparents,” Goodman said. “People who would’ve responded to an email.”

The case went to trial in federal court in Washington. After more than a day of deliberation, the jury returned guilty verdicts. Goodman was convicted under the federal Freedom of Access to Clinic Entrances or FACE Act, which criminalizes physically obstructing access to abortion clinics. U.S. Marshals were already waiting. Goodman handed over his wallet, watch, and phone and was led from the courtroom down a steel elevator into holding cells below.

He was sentenced to 27 months.

“Rather than feeling angry,” Goodman told me, “I started praying — for the jury, for the judge, for the prosecutors.”

He moved through holding units, transfer buses, and short stays before landing at the low-security federal prison in Danbury, Connecticut. On one transport, he briefly thought he was being taken to a maximum-security facility. “That was one of the freakiest moments,” he said.

Danbury housed a small cluster of January 6 defendants. Goodman was friendly with some of them, shared Bible study with a few, and passed time in the same yards and common rooms. But he made clear he inhabited a different world.

“I’m not a Trumper,” he said. “Rescue isn’t political. It’s human.”

Steve Bannon entered Goodman’s orbit the way most things do in prison — by rumor and proximity. Goodman first noticed him at Sunday Mass, sitting quietly in the back. Later, they crossed paths in the yard and the library and became friendly. Bannon was curious about Goodman’s case and the broader federal prosecutions of pro-life activists. He taught a packed civics class on American elections, using a Socratic style. “He listened,” Goodman said. “He was a good facilitator.”

Two months before his release, Goodman fell from his bunk. He suffered skull fractures, a concussion, and vertigo, and spent weeks immobilized. “It was a prison within a prison,” he said.

For Goodman, the pardon was never primarily about himself. “It meant my elderly friends might not die in prison,” he said. Several co-defendants were in their seventies.

The pardon also unsettled him. He does not see Trump as a consistently pro-life president, despite crediting him for the justices who overturned the Roe v. Wade decision whose anniversary was yesterday. And there was no obvious political upside: National pro-life leaders had not been clamoring for the prisoners’ release, and the cases were largely unknown outside activist circles.

“Strictly speaking,” Goodman said, “he had nothing to gain by pardoning us.” That fact gave him cautious hope — not necessarily in Trump, but in the possibility that disproportionate punishment might still be recognized as such.

Publicly, the pardon made him visible. Goodman appeared on Fox News and Bannon’s “War Room,” fielding questions about the cases and his time in prison. Privately, little changed.

I’ve always been struck by Goodman’s willingness to live with the consequences of ideas most people are content to argue about from a distance. He is unusually articulate for someone so uncompromising, capable of engaging opponents without caricature, of acknowledging moral complexity without retreating from his conclusions.

One afternoon this month, when I visited him, we drove past a Planned Parenthood clinic in rural Illinois. I hadn’t noticed it — a brick building set back from the road. He slowed and began praying aloud, his voice quiet but steady. When he finished, he accelerated back to speed without comment.

Since the pardon, Goodman has participated in two more rescues. He was arrested again last summer. In March, he was convicted in a separate case and placed on probation. Another rescue is being planned; he expects to be arrested.

Every arrest carries a second sentence — one served by his parents, his sister, his niece and nephew, whose presence and milestones he misses. When I asked whether his family understands his calling, he hesitated.

“I don’t know if I fully understand it myself,” Goodman said. “I don’t know how they could. There’s a mystery in love. You’re making a sacrifice, and you don’t always know if there’s fruit attached to it.”

I asked whether he ever thought he might be wrong. He didn’t hesitate. “Sure.” Sometimes, he said, he forces himself to consider alternatives: What if life begins at first breath instead of at conception? What if he had chosen another path — a quieter life, a career that didn’t involve prison cells and missed milestones?

“But I keep coming back to the same question,” he said. “Is the human life in the womb worthy of love?” He paused. “And if I’m wrong,” he added, “then at least I die trying to love.”

Daniel Allott is the former opinion editor at The Hill and the author of “On the Road in Trump’s America: A Journey into the Heart of a Divided Country.”

data.title: US, Ukraine, Russia to meet for talks in Abu Dhabi over war settlement
data.description:
Envoys from the U.S., Russia and Ukraine will meet in Abu Dhabi on Friday for security discussions aimed at ending Russia’s war in Ukraine, with the issue of territorial concessions as the main feature.

The trilateral talks come after Russian President Vladimir Putin met with President Trump’s top envoys, Steve Witkoff and his son-in-law Jared Kushner, for marathon talks in Moscow.

The talks began shortly before midnight and lasted about four hours. Also joining in the talks for the first time was Josh Gruenbaum, the head of the Federal Acquisition Service who was recently appointed as a senior adviser on Trump’s Board of Peace.

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky also met with Trump on Thursday on the sidelines of the World Economic Forum in Davos. Trump said it was a good meeting but that Ukraine and Russia had “a ways to go” before agreeing to end the war.

Trump has controversially invited Russia to join his Board of Peace, which was initially envisioned as a body to implement his peace plan in Gaza but has apparently expanded in scope.

Putin has said he’s ready to pay the $1 billion permanent membership fee from the nearly $245 billion Russian assets frozen in European banks – a move Western countries imposed in response to Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in Feb. 2022. 

“The remaining Russian assets frozen in the US can be used for rebuilding the territories damaged during the hostilities after a peace agreement is signed between Russia and Ukraine,” Yury Ushakov, a senior aide to Putin, wrote in a statement on Telegram.

Russia’s offer to use its frozen assets is viewed as a cynical ploy for Russia to assert its sovereignty over occupied Ukrainian territory. Ushakov said discussions on frozen Russian assets will continue between Witkoff and the head of Russia’s sovereign wealth fund, Kirill Dmitriev. 

Ushakov, however, raised doubt about reaching a major breakthrough with Ukraine, saying any settlement must address “the territorial issue based on the formula agreed upon in Anchorage,” referring to the August summit between the Russian president and Trump. 

The Russian position from Anchorage is understood as the Kremlin’s demand that Ukraine hand over about 20 percent of territory it controls in Ukraine’s east, called the Donbas. While Putin declared Russian annexation of that territory in 2022, Russian forces have failed to seize all of it from Ukrainian forces in a grinding, bloody war of attrition.

Zelensky maintains that it is against the Ukrainian constitution to give up sovereignty over Ukrainian territory, although he has signaled openness to freezing the front lines and non-recognition of Russian sovereignty. 

After meeting with Trump this week, Zelensky said “the documents aimed at ending this war are nearly ready,” but he warned that Putin has to be ready to compromise. 

“Everybody have to be ready, not only Ukraine,” he said.

Trump said he thinks Putin and Zelensky would both be “stupid” to not make a deal to end the war. 

“I believe they’re at a point now where they can come together and get a deal done. And if they don’t, they’re stupid.”

data.title: Lutnick: Getting booed by Al Gore 'greatest honor' of Davos trip
data.description:
Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said getting booed off the stage by former Vice President Al Gore during his speech at a dinner in Davos, Switzerland, was the “greatest honor” of his trip, mocking Gore’s reputation as a climate activist. 

“It was the greatest honor of my trip to Davos,” Lutnick said in an interview on Fox News’s “Jesse Waters Primetime.”

“I mean, what’s better than Al Gore – incapable of discourse, incapable of anything. And you all remember Al Gore told us, by today in 2025, the whole ice cap would be gone, and Greenland would be green,” Lutnick said.

The 200-person Davos dinner ended in uproar Thursday night after what The Financial Times called “combative remarks from Lutnick.” European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde reportedly walked out of the event and the whole thing wrapped up before dessert, according to Reuters. 

Lutnick pointed to an op-ed he wrote and published in The Financial Times as context to his speech. The piece criticized globalism and the World Economic Forum, the reason for the dinner, as a whole. 

“So at the end of this lefty– basically a very left set of talks where someone said we need a new form of capitalism, which I think is another way to say communism. And then they let me speak at the end,” Lutnick told Waters. “Then I give a three-minute talk, and I just talked about my op-ed.”

Gore told Mediaite that he wasn’t the only one to make his feelings about Lutnick’s speech known. although the Commerce Department told The Financial Times that Gore was the only one to boo Lutnick off the stage. 

“I sat and listened to his remarks,” Gore said in a statement to Mediaite Wednesday. “I didn’t interrupt him in any way. It’s no secret that I think this administration’s energy policy is insane. And at the end of his speech, I reacted with how I felt, and so did several others.”

data.title: Trump's coalition is falling apart
data.description:
Timing is everything. If the last presidential election were being held now instead of back in November 2024, it is not at all clear that President Trump would win — even against a less-than-ideal opponent.

This brings us to the central paradox of Trumpism: Trump is very good at campaigning and very bad at governing. This explains almost everything that has happened since he took office one year ago this week, including the nation’s rising consumption of Rolaids.

A new New York Times-Siena University poll shows that Trump’s approval rating strongly resembles what it looked like “in the run-up to his defeat in the 2020 presidential election.” Translation: America has seen this movie before, has been reminded of how it ends, and is already edging toward the exit.

“If anything,” the Times adds, “young and nonwhite voters are even likelier to disapprove of Mr. Trump than they were then, while he retains most of his support among older and white voters.”

This was so predictable that I predicted it myself back in December of 2024, noting that “holding this coalition together will be one of Trump’s greatest challenges in his second term,” and that “once you start governing and making decisions, you’re almost guaranteed to alienate some of the people who put you in office in the first place.”

This is always true of politicians. But Trump’s winning coalition was so sprawling and incoherent that pleasing one group would automatically enrage another.

This was especially true of the newer members of his coalition — minorities and young men who play video games for eight hours a day while listening to Joe Rogan explain the secrets of the universe. These folks have been fleeing for almost a year now, which is to say Trump’s political appeal operates like a poorly run corporate downsizing: last hired, first fired.

To be sure, much of this is simply the ultimate realization that Trump’s most electorally appealing promises — such as lowering grocery prices on day one — are never actually going to happen. Indeed, Trump’s policies — tariffs, for example — were almost custom-made to increase grocery prices, which is generally frowned upon by people who eat.

Trump’s true superpower, it turns out, only works when he is not actually in charge.

During campaigns, Trump thrives on attention. It doesn’t matter whether that attention is positive or negative. Governing, however, is different. When you’re in charge, attention comes with responsibility, and you can’t just shout, “THINGS ARE AMAZING!” forever.

Trump got to the top twice by ignoring wise old maxims, but this is where Trump should probably adhere to some cliches. For example, there is the saying: “If you’re in a hole, stop digging.”

Trump is digging many holes. With enthusiasm. Possibly with a backhoe.

Consider the last few weeks. Trump ended 2025 badly, weighed down by poor poll numbers, unfinished Jeffrey Epstein file releases, and Democratic gubernatorial victories.

Faced with this, he reached for his go-to solution: More Attention.

He opened the New Year by invading Venezuela and capturing Nicolás Maduro. From there, things escalated briskly. He defended an ICE agent who shot and killed a protester in Minneapolis named Renee Good. He threatened to invoke the Insurrection Act. He threatened to take Greenland — possibly by force. He threatened to slap tariffs on European allies over Greenland. He suggested his failure to win the Nobel Peace Prize justified taking Greenland. And he almost failed to issue any acknowledgment of Martin Luther King Jr. Day, waiting until bedtime to do so.

This is not so much a list of accomplishments as a cry for help.

The Times reports that voters now hold unfavorable views of Trump on “almost every issue tested in the poll,” including immigration, the economy, and foreign policy. And the new controversies — Venezuela, Greenland, Minneapolis — likely made things worse.

Each time Trump changes the subject, the new subject somehow manages to be more disagreeable than the last. It’s like channel-surfing, except every channel is CNN.

To be fair, there is a constituency for each of these moves. Somewhere out there, someone is nodding enthusiastically at every one of them. But taken together, they resemble a blitzkrieg against public opinion.

Trump is undeniably a man of action. He believes deeply in the idea that no publicity is bad publicity. He believes his presidency is like a bicycle: If it ever stopped moving, it would fall over.

Unfortunately, Trump also believes that moving fast in the wrong direction is better than standing still — even if that direction happens to be straight off a cliff.

Matt K. Lewis is a columnist, podcaster, and author of the books “Too Dumb to Fail” and “Filthy Rich Politicians.”

data.title: Former DC police officer Fanone: Jack Smith hearing 'kangaroo court'
data.description:
Former D.C. Metropolitan Police officer Michael Fanone on Thursday called the congressional hearing featuring former special counsel Jack Smith a “kangaroo court.”

“Listen, I came there to support Jack Smith,” Fanone told independent journalist Nicholas Ballasy. “In my eyes, you know, Jack Smith is a man of integrity who’s just doing his f—ing job, and I came there to show support. I didn’t learn a goddamn thing in this f—ing hearing that I didn’t already know. I just wanted to hear the words come from his mouth.”

“Other than that, it was exactly what I expected, a f—ing kangaroo court experience,” Fanone continued.

He called Congress “dysfunctional” no matter “what political party is in control” and called the Trump administration a disgrace. Fanone said the hearing was a “waste of time” and “four hours that, other than supporting Jack Smith, I wish I could f—ing get back.”

Ballasy asked Fanone what he thought of President Trump’s Truth Social post calling Smith “deranged.” Fanone replied that everything Trump “says about everyone else is exactly what he is.”

“Donald Trump is deranged,” he continued. “Donald Trump is insane, Donald Trump is incapable of compassion or empathy for others. He’s a sick c—.”

An altercation erupted between Fanone and conservative activist and conspiracy theorist Ivan Raiklin during the break in Smith’s testimony with the House Judiciary Committee.

“See how many people are restraining you, and look at me, totally in control over my mind and body,” Raiklin says to Fanone in video that captured the altercation.

Former Capitol Police officer Harry Dunn, who also served on Jan. 6, tried to pull Fanone away as uniformed officers stepped between Fanone and Raiklin.

“This guy has threatened my family, threatened my children, threatened to rape my children, you sick bastard,” Fanone says, pointing at Raiklin.

More U.S. Capitol Police officers were called to the room to step in, with some speaking with Raiklin. He later left the hearing room, but it’s unclear if he was ordered to leave or if he left on his own accord.

Fanone later appeared to say, “Go f— yourself,” when Rep. Troy Nehls (R-Texas) addressed former officers in the room and blamed Capitol Police leadership for the Capitol attack, not Trump.

During the hearing, Smith addressed Trump’s remarks about him. The president also posted on Truth Social that Smith was being “DECIMATED” and claimed he “destroyed many lives under the guise of legitimacy.” The former special counsel said he was not unnerved by comments “meant to intimidate.”

“We did our work pursuant to department policy — we followed the facts; we followed the law — and that process resulted in proof, beyond a reasonable doubt, that he committed serious crimes,” Smith said.

Smith led the investigations into Trump’s efforts to overturn the 2020 election and the mishandling of classified documents. Both cases were dropped when Trump returned to the White House.

data.title: Live updates: Trump-Carney tit-for-tat heats up; Minnesota 'economic blackout'
data.description:
A diplomatic tit-for-tat between President Trump and Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney is escalating, with the president disinviting Carney from his “Board of Peace” on Thursday night.

The move comes after Carney warned of a “rupture” in the trans-Atlantic alliance during his speech at Davos earlier this week, which prompted Trump to suggest Canada would not exist without its southern neighbor.

Carney, in a post on X on Wednesday, wrote, “Canada doesn’t ‘live because of the United States.’ Canada thrives because we are Canadian.”

Trump addressed Carney in a post on Truth Social Thursday night, saying Canada was no longer invited to join “the most prestigious Board of Leaders ever assembled, at any time.” Carney had previously said Canada was considering the invitation.

In Minnesota, civic leaders and labor unions are hosting a day of action on Friday amid protests over ongoing federal immigration action across the state. Many businesses plan to close as organizers encourage Minnesotans to stay home for an economic blackout.

“It is time to suspend the normal order of business to demand immediate cessation of ICE actions in (Minnesota), accountability for federal agents who have caused loss of life and abuse to Minnesota residents and call for Congress to immediately intervene,” a statement on the event website read.

The annual March for Life will take place at midday on the National Mall in Washington. Vice President Vance is expected to address the march, along with Rep. Chris Smith (R-N.J.).

Follow live below.

data.title: Contractor indicted in leak case tied to search of Washington Post reporter's home
data.description:
A government contractor who allegedly leaked classified information in a case linked to the search of a Washington Post journalist’s home was indicted by a grand jury on Thursday. 

Aurelio Luis Perez-Lugones is charged with five counts of unlawfully transmitting and one count of unlawfully retaining classified national defense information, the Justice Department (DOJ) said. 

Perez-Lugones allegedly “repeatedly accessed classified reports” and removed them from his sensitive compartmented information facility (SCIF) to then share with a reporter, according to the DOJ’s release. 

“Illegally disclosing classified defense information is a grave crime against America that puts both our national security and the lives of our military heroes at risk,” Attorney General Pam Bondi said in the release. “This Department of Justice will remain ever-vigilant in protecting the integrity of America’s classified intelligence.”

The DOJ also said that Perez-Lugones had previously been charged with retaining national defense information. 

The indictment follows the FBI’s search of Post reporter Hannah Natanson’s Virginia home. Federal agents took several devices, including two phones, two laptops, a recorder, a portable hard drive and a Garmin watch, according to the newspaper.

Natanson has reported on the Trump administration’s efforts to cut down on government spending by shrinking the federal workforce. 

On Wednesday, the Post submitted a request to a judge to order the return of these materials. 

The newspaper wrote in its request that this seizure “violates the Constitution’s protections for free speech and a free press and should not be allowed to stand.”

“It is a prior restraint and a violation of the reporter’s privilege that flouts the First Amendment and ignores federal statutory safeguards for journalists,” the Post wrote. “The seizure chills speech, cripples reporting, and inflicts irreparable harm every day the government keeps its hands on protected material.”

data.title: Trump's overreaction to charges of fraud is harming thousands — and that's the goal
data.description:
Earlier this month, there was rare, broad consensus at a House subcommittee hearing that child care is critically important to children, families, employers and our economy — and that the system we have right now does not meet those stakeholders’ needs.  

I testified that we can’t build a system where families can find and afford quality child care, and early educators are well-compensated for skillfully caring for our youngest children, without robust public investments.

Unfortunately, the administration’s recent actions freezing federal child care funding threaten to further destabilize our fragile and underfunded system.

In response to allegations of fraud from a right-wing influencer who visited child care providers in Minnesota demanding to know “where is the money going,” the Trump administration froze federal child care funds to the entire state of Minnesota. In addition, the administration froze child care and human services funding to California, New York, Illinois and Colorado. Fortunately, a federal court temporarily blocked the administration’s actions in all five states.  

But the administration wasn’t finished.  

It then announced that it would require all states to provide additional information in order to receive federal child care funds — what it termed “defend the spend.” The administration also proposed to eliminate portions of a regulation that capped child care  payments for families and made payments more predictable and stable for providers. What’s more, President Trump sent ICE agents to Minnesota and other states, with violent and tragic results, and announced that the Department of Justice will create a new position charged with investigating fraud.  

As a parent, I can tell you the administration’s attack on our already precarious child care system breaks my heart. And as an expert on child care and early learning, I know that such an outsized response will cause serious harm to parents, providers and communities.  

If California, Colorado, Illinois, Minnesota and New York do not receive federal child care and social services funding, about 300,000 children could be directly impacted. Thousands of parents would lose the child care they need to work or look for work. Businesses would lose productivity and revenues. Child care providers may go out of business.  And even though the funding freeze has been enjoined, more “concerned citizens” have started  pounding on child care providers’ doors in Minnesota, and reportedly in Ohio, Washington state and Maine. 

To be sure, for the moment, a court has forbidden the administration to withhold funds. But, in another sense, harm has already been done. Fear and uncertainty, among parents, providers and communities, is rampant. Families don’t know if they will be able to take their children to child care. Providers don’t know if they will be able to pay rent and make payroll.

A life has been lost. An army of vigilantes feels it has been given license to terrorize child care providers, early educators and the children in their care. And communities are bracing for worse to come.   

It’s telling that while the administration responded to unproven allegations from a conservative YouTuber with lightning speed, it has done nothing over the past year to make the child care system work any better for families, providers or teachers. In fact, it’s taken steps at every turn to make the child-care crisis worse. It’s almost as though the administration’s true goals have nothing to do with building a system that works for everyone, and everything to do with punishing political opponents, demonizing immigrants, and cutting basic needs programs that struggling families rely on.  

We need to strengthen and build the child-care system we all need and deserve — not harm thousands of children, families, providers and communities by threatening to freeze funding.

Amy K. Matsui, JD, is vice president for child care and income security at the National Women’s Law Center, and a Public Voices Fellow of the OpEd Project in Partnership with the National Black Child Development Institute.

data.title: Watch live: Vance, Johnson speak at March for Life rally
data.description:
Vice President Vance and Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) are scheduled to speak Friday at the annual March for Life rally in Washington D.C.

Vance and President Trump spoke to anti-abortion advocates at last year’s event. It has been held every year since 1974.

Watch Friday’s remarks, scheduled to begin at 12 p.m. EST, in the player above.

data.description:
